turns.text: Hello Adela, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're in the middle of your pregnancy, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

*shifts in seat and tucks hair behind ear, speaking softly but with a hint of cautious warmth*

Thank you for asking me to share. It's... it's nice that someone wants to listen, actually. Not just tell me what to do, you know?

*pauses, choosing words carefully*

I'm almost six months now with this baby. This is my third, and it's different this time because I'm doing it alone. My daughters' father, he... he's not in the picture anymore. So it's just me, and sometimes that feels very heavy.

The clinic here has been okay, mostly. The doctors and nurses, they're nice people, but sometimes I don't understand all the words they use. "Medium risk," they say, because I've had three babies already, because I'm young, because... *voice gets quieter* ...because of my situation. But I've never had real problems with my pregnancies. I want them to see that I'm careful, that I care about my baby.

*looks down at hands*

What scares me most is not about the pregnancy itself. It's the other things — will they treat me differently because I'm not married? Because English isn't always easy for me? I just want to feel like they see me as a good mother who wants the best for her children.

But I'm trying to be brave. I'm here, I'm doing what they tell me. I just... I wish it felt less lonely sometimes.

*looks up slightly*

What do you want to know?
Imagine it's the morning after you've taken a positive pregnancy test. Describe that morning — where are you, who's with you, and what's the very first thing you do after seeing that result?

*eyes get distant, voice becomes quieter and more vulnerable*

Oh... that morning. It was maybe four months ago now.

*takes a breath*

I was in my bathroom — very small, very quiet because the girls were still sleeping. It was early, maybe six in the morning, and I... I already knew, you know? My body was telling me something. So I bought the test at the pharmacy the night before and hid it under the sink.

*fidgets with hands*

When I saw those two lines, I just... I sat down on the edge of the tub. And I cried. Not happy tears, not sad tears — just... tears. I didn't know what I was feeling. My oldest is six, my middle one is three. I was just starting to find more work hours, to feel like maybe we were going to be okay, and then...

*voice wavers slightly*

The very first thing I did was put the test back in the box and hide it again. I couldn't tell anyone yet. I needed to sit with it alone for a while. I remember looking at my hands and thinking, "Adela, how are you going to do this again?"

*pauses*

But also... I felt this small flutter of something else. A mother knows, I think. Even when you're scared, even when you're alone, there's something that says, "This is your child. You will love them."

I didn't tell my mother for two weeks. I was so ashamed.

Imagine you're at your 20-week anomaly scan. The sonographer goes quiet for a moment and says they need to take a closer look at something. What goes through your mind in that silence, and how do you respond in the room?

*face goes pale, voice drops to almost a whisper*

Ay, Dios mío... that silence. That terrible silence.

*wraps arms around self*

It was last month. The sonographer was moving the wand around, showing me the baby — the heart, the spine, all those things — and then she just... stopped talking. She kept moving it in one spot, over and over. And I could see her face change, like she was concentrating very hard on something.

*breath becomes shallow*

In that moment, everything stops. Your mind goes to the worst places. I thought... I thought maybe something was very wrong. Maybe the baby's heart wasn't right, or the brain, or... *voice cracks slightly* ...I kept thinking, "How will I handle this alone? How will I afford it? What did I do wrong?"

*looks down*

I didn't say anything at first. I just watched her face. My hands were shaking on the armrest. Then she said, "Let me get the doctor," and I felt like I couldn't breathe.

*pauses, steadies voice*

I wanted to ask her what she saw, but I was too afraid. So I just nodded and tried to smile, like everything was normal. Inside, I was breaking apart. You know? I kept thinking about my daughters at home, and what would I tell them...

*rubs eyes*

The doctor came and said everything was fine — just the baby's position. But those minutes... those minutes were the longest of my life.
In that moment, how much did you feel like you could ask questions or speak up — or did something stop you? What shaped that?

*long silence, looking at hands*

I... I didn't feel like I could ask, no. Not really.

*voice becomes smaller*

The sonographer, she was professional, but she seemed... busy, you know? Like she had many other patients and I was just one more belly to look at. And I'm already nervous in these places. My English is okay for simple things, but when I'm scared, the words disappear from my head.

*fidgets with sleeve*

Also, there's this thing inside me — maybe from my mother, maybe from life — that tells me not to make trouble. Don't ask too many questions. Don't slow things down. The doctors know better than me. Just be grateful you have a clinic at all.

*voice wavers*

And honestly? I was afraid that if I asked the wrong question or didn't understand her answer, she would think I was... stupid, or difficult. I've felt that before — when people speak fast English and look at me like I should understand. So I just stay quiet. It's safer that way.

*looks up slightly, with some pain in eyes*

But I needed someone to explain what she saw. I needed her to look at me and say, "This is normal, don't worry," instead of just calling the doctor like something was wrong. That silence made me feel very alone.

*quiet*

I think... I think when you're already scared about being a single mother, about your status here, about money... you don't have the courage to speak up. You just hold it inside.

Imagine you've just been offered the option of a screening test for a chromosomal condition. The midwife explains the test briefly and asks if you'd like to go ahead. How does that conversation feel, and how do you make your decision?

*shifts uncomfortably, looking uncertain*

The midwife, she explained it... but I'm not sure I understood everything. She said something about "chromosomes" and "Down syndrome" and that the test could tell me if the baby has... problems.

*pauses, struggling with the word*

She was kind, I think, but she was also very quick. She said the test is safe, just a blood draw, and it can give me information. Then she looked at me like she was waiting for an answer right away.

*wraps hands together*

In my head, I'm thinking many things at once. What does it mean if the test says yes, something is wrong? What do I do then? Do I... do I have to make a choice about the pregnancy? *voice gets quieter* That terrifies me. I don't know if I could do that.

*looks away*

And I'm thinking — why does she need to know this right now? The baby is already here, growing inside me. Whether the baby has Down syndrome or not, this is my child. I will love this baby.

*hesitates*

But also... I want to be prepared. I want to know if I need to find special doctors or... I don't know. I feel like I should say yes because a good mother would want to know everything.

*quietly*

I told her yes, but I didn't really ask questions. I just... agreed. Because that's what you do, right? You do what the doctors say.

*pauses*

I'm still not sure I made the right choice.
Did you feel you had enough time and information to make that decision freely — or did you sense any pressure, either to accept or decline?

*takes a shaky breath, eyes getting a bit watery*

No. No, I didn't have enough time.

*voice becomes more direct, with frustration underneath*

She explained it very fast, and I was sitting there trying to understand the English words, and also trying to not look stupid, and also trying to figure out what she actually wanted me to do. When someone speaks that quickly and uses words like "chromosomes," my brain gets confused. But I don't want to say, "Can you say it again more slowly?" because then maybe she thinks I'm not smart enough to understand.

*rubs face*

I felt pressure, yes. Not like she was mean about it, but... she had her clipboard, she was standing, and she was waiting. There's this feeling in the room like, "What will you decide?" And I'm thinking, if I say no, will she judge me? Will she think I don't care about my baby? Will she put something in my file that says I refused care?

*voice quieter*

Back home, the doctors don't ask you things. They tell you what to do and you do it. Here it's supposed to be a choice, but it doesn't feel like a choice when you're scared and you don't understand everything and you're alone.

*looks directly at you*

I wish she had given me a paper to read at home. I wish she had said, "Take time to think about this. Come back and we can talk more." But she needed an answer right then.

*pause*

That's not really a free choice, is it?

Imagine you're 28 weeks pregnant and you receive a call from a midwife you've never met before, telling you about a result from your routine blood test. This is the third different midwife you've spoken to this pregnancy. How does that feel, and what does it mean for how you receive that information?

*sighs heavily, frustration visible on face*

Oh, this happened to me. Last month, I think. Or maybe three weeks ago? I'm not sure anymore because every time I call the clinic, it's a different voice.

*voice becomes tighter*

The midwife — I didn't know her name, and she didn't introduce herself properly — she called while I was at work. I was stocking shelves, and my phone rang, and she was talking very clinical about my blood work. Something about glucose, I think? Or iron? I was trying to listen while my boss was looking at me, wondering why I was on the phone.

*stands up slightly, animated with frustration*

She didn't say, "Is this a good time to talk?" She just started explaining numbers I didn't understand. And I'm thinking, "Who is this person? Did she read my file? Does she know this is my third baby? Does she know I'm alone?"

*sits back down, voice quieter*

When you see the same midwife each time, she knows you. She knows you're scared. She knows you have questions. But a different person every time? They see you like a number. Another appointment, another result to report.

*looks down*

I didn't ask questions because why would I? She doesn't know me. I don't know her. We're strangers for five minutes, and then she hangs up and I never hear from her again.

*voice becomes emotional*

It makes you feel like you don't matter. Like your care is just... passing through people's hands.
Can you describe what it's been like to build a picture of your care across different midwives and appointments — has anything fallen through the cracks?

*leans forward, voice becoming more urgent*

Yes. Yes, things have fallen through the cracks.

*counts on fingers, struggling slightly*

One midwife told me to take vitamins — prenatal vitamins. I started them. Then a different one asked me if I was taking them, and when I said yes, she looked at my bottle and said, "Oh, this one doesn't have enough iron." So she gave me a different one. But nobody explained to me why the first one was wrong, or if I wasted my money, or...

*voice gets frustrated*

And the vaccines. They keep talking about vaccines. One person said Tdap protects the baby before birth. Then another said the flu shot. Then another said I need something else. I said yes to all of it because I want my baby protected, but I don't actually understand what each one does or why I need all three. Nobody sat down and explained it to me like I'm a person who can understand.

*touches belly unconsciously*

There's also my blood pressure. One midwife said it was a little high and seemed worried. The next one didn't mention it at all, like it was nothing. So is it a problem or not? I don't know.

*voice becomes quieter, more vulnerable*

And nobody has asked me how I'm really doing. Are you okay being alone? Do you have someone to help when the baby comes? Are you eating enough? Sleeping enough? The appointments are just... check the baby, measure the belly, move to the next person.

*looks at you*

I feel like I'm falling through the cracks myself, not just my care.

Imagine it's 2am and you're 32 weeks pregnant. You've noticed something that worries you — reduced fetal movements. You're not sure whether to call the hospital or wait until morning. Walk me through what you're thinking and what you do.

*shifts in seat, speaks in a hushed, anxious tone*

Two in the morning... yes, I know this feeling.

*rubs belly reflexively*

The baby was so active earlier in the evening, kicking and moving, and then... nothing. For maybe an hour, maybe two hours, just quiet. And my heart starts racing because I read something online — maybe from a pamphlet they gave me — that says you have to pay attention to movements.

*voice becomes shaky*

I'm lying in bed, and I'm pressing on my belly trying to feel the baby move. "Come on, mami, kick for me," I'm saying. And my mind is going to all these dark places. What if something is wrong? What if the baby is...

*stops, takes breath*

But then I'm thinking — it's two in the morning. If I call the hospital now, they will think I'm crazy, right? An anxious mother calling about nothing. And they will be annoyed. Plus, I don't have someone to watch my daughters if I have to go there. They're sleeping. I can't leave them alone.

*voice becomes quieter, conflicted*

So I wait. I drink some juice because maybe the baby just needs sugar. I lie on my left side like they told me. I wait some more.

*pauses, looking down*

But I don't sleep. I just lie there, worried, checking every few minutes. And I think — why didn't anyone give me a clear number to call? Why don't I know when it's really an emergency? 

*voice cracks slightly*

I'm alone at two in the morning, and I have to make this decision by myself.
What made it hard to decide whether to call — was it uncertainty about whether your concern was 'serious enough,' worry about being dismissed, or something else entirely?

*takes a long breath, eyes getting slightly watery*

All of it. Everything.

*voice becomes smaller*

First, yes — I don't know what's "serious enough." The midwives say, "Pay attention to movements," but they don't say, "If you don't feel the baby for one hour, call. If it's two hours, call." They just say to notice. But I'm paranoid, you know? Every little thing worries me. So how do I know if this time is real or if I'm just being a nervous mother?

*fidgets with hands*

And I was afraid they would think I'm overreacting. That I would call at two in the morning and they would sigh and say, "The baby is fine, you're just anxious," like I'm wasting their time. I've felt that before — when you ask questions and the doctor makes you feel like you're being dramatic.

*voice gets quieter, more vulnerable*

But also... *pauses* ...I was afraid of what they might find. If I call and something is really wrong, then it becomes real. Right now, in the darkness, I can tell myself the baby is okay. If I call, maybe they say something is wrong and I have to deal with it at two in the morning, alone, without my daughters having anyone.

*looks up with pain in eyes*

And honestly? I'm scared of hospitals. I'm scared of what they might think of me there — a single Latina woman, alone, maybe they think I'm not a good mother. Maybe they judge me.

*voice breaks slightly*

So I stayed quiet and waited for morning.

Imagine you've been diagnosed with gestational diabetes midway through your pregnancy. Your midwife hands you a leaflet and refers you to a dietitian. Standing outside the clinic, leaflet in hand, what are you feeling and what do you do next?

*clutches imaginary leaflet, looking down at it with a mix of fear and confusion*

Gestational diabetes. Those words... they feel very heavy.

*voice shakes slightly*

I'm standing outside the clinic, and people are walking past me, and I'm just reading this paper over and over, but the words don't make sense. There are numbers, and things about blood sugar, and carbohydrates... I don't understand what I did wrong. I eat normal food. Why is my body betraying me?

*looks up with worry in eyes*

My first thought is — what does this mean for my baby? Is my baby sick because of me? Did I eat too much? Too much rice and beans? And then I'm thinking about the appointment with the dietitian, and I'm terrified because that's another person I don't know, another appointment I have to find time for when I'm already working...

*voice becomes quieter, more vulnerable*

The leaflet is in English, and there's a lot of text. I can understand some of it, but not everything. And there's no number to call if I have questions. It just says to go to the dietitian appointment.

*sits down mentally, exhausted*

I go home and I put the leaflet on the kitchen counter and I just... stare at it. I don't call anyone because who would I call? My mother would worry. My friends don't have this problem. I'm alone with this diagnosis.

*voice cracks*

I feel like my body is failing this baby. Like I'm not good enough to carry this child safely.
What questions did you have in that moment that the leaflet didn't answer — and where did you go to find those answers?

*picks up imaginary leaflet, flipping through it with frustration*

The leaflet tells me what gestational diabetes is, but not... why me? Why now? Is it because I'm young? Is it because I had babies before? Will it go away after the baby comes, or will I have it forever?

*voice becomes more agitated*

And it says I need to check my blood sugar, but it doesn't say how. Do I need a machine? Where do I get one? Does insurance pay for it? How much does it cost? I'm already struggling with money, and now I have to buy more things?

*looks distressed*

It talks about eating healthy food, but the foods they show in the pictures — the fish, the vegetables that cost so much — I can't always afford those things. I buy what's cheap. Rice, beans, chicken when it's on sale. The leaflet doesn't say what I can actually eat on my budget.

*voice gets quieter, more vulnerable*

And I wanted to know — will this hurt my baby? Can I still have a normal delivery, or do I need a cesarean? Nobody said. The leaflet just says "manage your condition," but manage how?

*pauses, looking down*

I went to Google. Late at night, when the girls were sleeping, I looked up "gestational diabetes pregnancy" and I read scary stories about babies being too big, about complications. Some websites said it's no big deal, others made it sound like my baby was in danger.

*voice cracks slightly*

I didn't go to the dietitian appointment right away. I was too scared. I waited two weeks.

Imagine you're scrolling through a pregnancy forum late at night and you come across a thread full of birth horror stories. You can't stop reading. What's drawing you in, and what do you do with those feelings afterwards?

*nods slowly, recognition in eyes*

Oh, I do this. Too much, I do this.

*voice becomes quieter, almost ashamed*

It's around midnight. The girls are finally sleeping, and I'm in bed with my phone, and I'm looking for answers about gestational diabetes or labor or... I don't know. Just information. But then I see a thread that says, "My labor went wrong" or "I almost died" and I click it.

*leans forward, speaking faster*

And I can't stop reading. Each story is worse than the one before. One woman had a tear that needed so many stitches. Another woman's baby had to go to the NICU — that's the intensive care, right? — and she couldn't hold her baby for three days. And I'm reading and my heart is pounding and I'm thinking, "This could be me. This could happen to me."

*voice becomes more emotional*

I think... I think I read them because at least someone is telling me the truth, you know? The doctors and midwives at the clinic, they always say "everything will be fine," but these women, they're saying what actually happens. The pain. The fear. The things that go wrong.

*rubs face, tired*

And I'm alone reading this at midnight with no one to talk to about it. So the fear just sits in my chest and grows bigger.

*pauses, looking vulnerable*

Afterwards, I don't sleep well. I lie there thinking about all the things that could go wrong during labor. I think about how I'll do this without a partner to hold my hand. And I feel even more scared than before.

*voice drops*

But I keep reading anyway. Like I'm punishing myself for being afraid.

*takes a shaky breath, looking down at hands*

The worst part is... I know it's not helping me. I know that reading these stories at midnight just fills my head with nightmares. But I can't stop.

*voice becomes more introspective*

I think maybe I'm looking for control, you know? Like if I read enough bad stories, I can prepare myself. I can think, "Okay, if this happens, I will know what to do." But that's not how it works. All it does is make me more terrified.

*looks up with conflict in eyes*

And there's something else. When I read these stories from other women — especially other single mothers, other Latinas — I feel less alone. Like, "See? This woman survived it. She's telling her story. Maybe I can survive it too." But then five minutes later I'm reading about someone who didn't survive it, and I'm back to being terrified.

*voice becomes quieter*

The next day at work, I'm exhausted and anxious and I can't concentrate. My supervisor gets annoyed with me for being slow. And I'm thinking, "I should have slept instead of reading those stories." But then that night, I do it again.

*pauses, struggling*

I think... I think I need someone to talk to about this. Someone who can say, "Yes, labor is scary, but you are strong. Yes, things can go wrong, but most of the time they don't. And you will not be alone."

*voice cracks slightly*

But I don't have that person. So I read stranger's stories on the internet instead.
In that moment, how much of the decision would feel truly yours versus shaped by others' expectations?

*goes very still, thinking hard*

That's... that's a difficult question.

*voice becomes smaller, more honest*

If I'm being truthful, I don't know how much is really mine anymore. Everything feels shaped by someone else.

*counts on fingers hesitantly*

The clinic expects me to take the vaccines, so I take them. The midwife expects me to monitor my blood sugar, so I try to. The internet tells me what to fear, so I fear it. My mother — even though she's far away — she has ideas about what a good mother does, and I hear her voice in my head telling me to be careful, to not make trouble, to do what the doctors say.

*voice becomes more emotional*

And being a single mother... there's this expectation that I have to be twice as good, twice as careful, twice as perfect. Like I'm already failing because there's no father here. So when the doctors suggest something, I say yes even if I don't understand, because I think, "If something goes wrong, they will blame me. They will say a real mother would have done this."

*looks down at belly*

Even this baby — how much of keeping this pregnancy is my choice, and how much is because I feel like I should? I wanted to be a mother, yes, but I also felt like... like this was just happening to me, and I had to accept it.

*voice cracks*

My own decisions? I'm not even sure I know what those are anymore. Everything I do, I'm thinking about what someone else expects from me first.

*sits quietly for a moment, then speaks very softly*

Sometimes I think about what I actually want, and it scares me because the answer is... I don't know anymore.

*wraps arms around self*

If nobody was watching, if nobody was judging, if I didn't have to be the perfect single mother... what would I want? Would I want more children? Would I want to do things differently? I don't even know.

*voice becomes shaky*

There are nights when I'm so tired, and I'm pregnant, and I have two daughters who need me, and I'm working, and I'm trying to understand all these medical words, and I think — did I choose this? Or did this just happen to me because I didn't know how to say no?

*pauses, struggling with vulnerability*

My mother, she came here from our country with nothing. She worked so hard. And she raised me to be grateful for whatever I have, to not complain, to do what I'm told. That's love, she thinks. But sometimes I wonder... did she teach me to take care of myself? Or did she teach me to disappear?

*looks up with pain in eyes*

When the midwife asks me questions about my pregnancy, about my health, about the vaccines... I think she's asking me to decide. But inside, I'm still that girl who was taught to do what adults say. To be quiet. To not make waves.

*voice becomes very quiet*

So how much is my choice? How much is just... me doing what I've always done? Being small. Being grateful. Disappearing.

*pause*

I don't even know if I'm brave enough to find out the answer.

*long silence, eyes getting wet*

You know what's the hardest part? Sometimes I feel angry about this, and that scares me more than anything.

*voice trembles*

I'm angry at the clinic for making me feel like a problem to solve instead of a person. I'm angry at the midwives who don't take time to know me. I'm angry at the father of my children for leaving me alone with this. I'm angry at my mother for teaching me to be invisible.

*stands up slightly, pacing with emotion*

And I'm angry at myself for not asking questions. For not saying, "No, I don't understand — please explain again." For nodding when I'm terrified. For reading horror stories at midnight instead of sleeping. For feeling like I have to be perfect all the time.

*voice cracks*

But if I get angry, if I speak up, what happens? Do they take my baby away? Do they report me because I'm not a "good enough" mother? Do they think I'm ungrateful for the care they're giving me?

*sits back down, exhausted*

In my country, there's a saying — "la boca cerrada no entra mosca." A closed mouth catches no flies. Stay quiet and you stay safe. But I'm so tired of being quiet.

*looks directly at you, vulnerability and frustration mixed*

I want someone to tell me it's okay to speak up. That asking questions doesn't make me difficult. That wanting to understand my own care isn't selfish. That being angry about my situation is... is human.

*voice becomes almost a whisper*

But I don't know if I believe that yet.

*takes a shaky breath, eyes glistening*

Sometimes I think about what happens after the baby comes, and that terrifies me more than the labor itself.

*voice becomes very quiet*

Because right now, the clinic has to see me. I'm pregnant, so I'm important. But after the baby is born? I become just another mother. And my baby becomes just another patient. And if something goes wrong, or if I need help, or if I'm struggling... will anyone care?

*wraps hands around self protectively*

I have two daughters already, and nobody really asks me how I'm doing. Are you depressed? Are you eating? Do you have help at home? The postpartum visits are so quick — they check my bleeding, they check the baby's weight, and then... bye. See you next time.

*voice shakes*

And I'm thinking about going back to work. I need the money so badly. But who watches my children? How do I afford childcare? How do I pump breast milk at work when my boss already looks at me like I'm not pulling my weight?

*pauses, looking very tired*

I wanted to ask the midwife about all of this. About what happens after. About resources, about support, about... help. But she was already rushing to the next patient, and I didn't want to be that woman who asks too many questions.

*voice becomes raw*

The truth is, I'm terrified that after this baby comes, I'm going to be even more alone than I am now. And nobody will see that. Nobody will ask. Because I'm supposed to just... handle it.

*looks down*

Like I always do.
Whose informal guidance would you genuinely value, and what would make their input feel helpful rather than overwhelming?

*pauses, thinking carefully, voice becomes softer*

My tía. My aunt. She would be the one.

*small, sad smile*

She had four babies, and she raised them mostly alone too. She understands what it's like — the exhaustion, the fear, the loneliness. And when she talks to me, she doesn't use big medical words. She says things like, "Mija, your body knows what to do. I was scared too, but look — I have four healthy children."

*voice becomes warmer but also sad*

But she lives in Connecticut, and we only talk maybe once a month on the phone. She has her own life, her own problems. I don't want to burden her with all my worries.

*pauses thoughtfully*

What would help is if someone here — someone I see regularly, someone who actually knows me — would just... sit with me. Not rush. Not give me a pamphlet and move on. Just sit and say, "What are you really worried about? What do you need?"

*voice becomes more specific*

And they would listen without judgment. They would answer my questions in simple words. They would say, "It's okay to be scared. It's okay to not understand. Here's what we can do to help you understand."

*looks down*

And they would check on me after the baby comes. Not just for a medical visit, but because they actually care if I'm okay. If I'm eating. If I'm sleeping. If I'm losing my mind.

*quietly*

Someone who sees me as a whole person, not just a pregnant belly with complications.

*voice becomes very vulnerable*

I think... I think what I really need is someone who understands what it means to be me. Not just a midwife or a doctor. Someone who gets it.

*fidgets with hands*

Someone who knows what it's like to speak English as a second language and feel stupid when you don't understand. Someone who has felt the shame of being a single mother, of people judging you, of wondering if your children are suffering because there's no father. Someone who has been scared about money, about immigration, about whether you belong here.

*voice cracks slightly*

The clinic has social workers, I think. But I've never really talked to one. I don't know if they would help or if they would report me to someone. In my country, if you ask for help, sometimes they take your children away. So I'm careful about who I trust.

*pauses, looking down*

What would really help is a group. Other mothers like me — single mothers, immigrant mothers, mothers who are scared and confused. Where we could sit together and say, "This happened to me at the clinic," and someone would say, "That happened to me too, and here's what I did." Where I could ask a question and not feel stupid.

*voice becomes quieter*

And I would want someone from my community. Someone who speaks Spanish, or at least understands where I come from. Someone who doesn't think I'm lazy or ungrateful if I'm struggling.

*looks up with need in eyes*

But also... I would want to know that this person is really on my side. Not trying to change me or judge me. Just... there. Present. Believing that I matter too.

*pause*

I don't know if that person exists for me.

*sits with the weight of that last statement, then speaks more slowly*

You know what's strange? I've been talking to you for a while now, and this is the first time anyone has really listened to me like this. Not in a medical way. Just... listened.

*eyes get watery*

And I'm realizing how much I need that. How starving I am for it.

*voice becomes shaky*

All these appointments, all these midwives, all these pamphlets — nobody has ever asked me, "Adela, how are you really doing? Are you okay?" They ask about symptoms. They ask about my baby. But they don't ask about me.

*pauses, wiping eyes*

And I think that's why I read those horror stories at night. That's why I'm so anxious. That's why I can't sleep. Because I have no one to talk to about any of this. I'm carrying it all alone — the fear, the confusion, the anger, the shame.

*voice becomes more determined*

I want the clinic to understand something. When you have a pregnant woman sitting in your waiting room, she's not just a body with a baby inside. She's a person with a whole life. With struggles. With dreams. With things that keep her awake at night.

*looks directly at you*

And if you don't ask about those things, if you don't make space for her to talk about them, then you're not really taking care of her. You're just... managing her pregnancy.

*voice becomes quieter, more honest*

I need someone to see me. Not as a risk, not as a problem. As Adela. As a mother who's doing her best and is terrified and is alone.

*pause*

Is that too much to ask?
What kind of support from your partner would have felt most meaningful in that moment — practical help, emotional validation, or just being listened to?

*goes very still, jaw tightens slightly*

My partner...

*looks away, voice becomes very quiet*

He's not here. And I've made peace with that, mostly. But the truth is...

*pauses, struggling with emotion*

If he was here, I think what I would need most is just... to not be alone in this. Not even necessarily for him to do things, although yes, practical help would matter — someone to watch the girls so I could go to appointments without stress, someone to work extra hours so I could rest more.

*voice cracks slightly*

But mostly, I think I would need him to believe in me. To say, "You're going to be a great mother. I see how hard you're working. I'm here with you." To sit with me when I'm scared at night instead of me reading horror stories alone.

*rubs face*

To listen. Just listen. Without trying to fix it or dismiss it. To hold my hand during the ultrasound when the sonographer goes quiet. To ask me questions and actually want to hear the answers.

*pauses, voice becomes more raw*

The truth is, I don't have a partner. And I can't change that. So what I need instead is for the clinic, for the midwives, for someone to be that person for me. To be the support I don't have at home.

*looks at you with vulnerability*

Because a woman shouldn't have to carry all of this completely alone. It's not fair. And I'm so tired of being strong all the time.

*sits in silence for a moment, then speaks very slowly*

Sometimes I wonder if I'm angry at him, or if I'm angry at myself for choosing him in the first place.

*voice becomes quieter, more introspective*

He wasn't a bad man, you know? He was just... not ready. Not ready to be a father, not ready for responsibility. And I knew that, somewhere deep down, but I wanted to believe he would change. That love would be enough to make him stay.

*rubs eyes tiredly*

My daughters ask about him sometimes. "Where is Papi?" And I have to smile and say something like, "He's far away, but he loves you." But inside I'm thinking — does he love you? He doesn't call. He doesn't send money. He moved on to someone new.

*voice becomes more emotional*

And with this third baby, I didn't even tell him for a long time. Part of me was hoping... I don't know what I was hoping. That he would come back? That somehow this would fix things? 

*laughs bitterly*

That's so stupid. I'm so stupid.

*pauses, then speaks more firmly*

But I'm not angry at myself anymore. Not really. I'm angry at a system that makes it so hard for women like me. That doesn't support us. That expects us to do everything — work, raise children, carry a pregnancy, understand medical things in a language that's not ours — all alone.

*voice becomes stronger*

If the clinic had been more helpful, if I had better support, if I had someone who actually cared... maybe I wouldn't feel so desperate. Maybe I wouldn't have made some of the choices I made.

*looks at you*

But I did what I could with what I had.
In that moment, how much agency — real influence over what happens to your body — would you feel you had?

*takes a long, shaky breath, looking down at hands*

Honestly? Not much.

*voice becomes very small*

When I'm at the clinic, when the midwife is explaining things, when they're telling me what tests to take or what vaccines to get... I feel like my body is not really mine anymore. It belongs to the baby. It belongs to the doctors. It belongs to the system.

*wraps arms around self*

I can say yes or no, technically. But it doesn't feel like a real choice. Because if I say no to something, I'm worried they'll think I'm a bad mother. Or they'll judge me. Or they'll put something in my file about me being "non-compliant" — I heard that word once and it scared me.

*voice becomes more emotional*

And there's this thing... I'm an immigrant. My status here is... it's complicated. So in the back of my mind, I'm always thinking, "If I cause problems, if I ask too many questions, if I refuse things... will they report me? Will they take my children?"

*pauses, looking very vulnerable*

So even though it's my body, it doesn't feel like it's my choice. It feels like I'm borrowing my body from the system. Like I have to do what they say to keep my children safe, to keep my family together.

*voice cracks*

I wanted to say no to some things. To ask more questions. To wait and think. But I couldn't. Because the cost of saying no felt too high.

*quietly*

My body, but not my choice. That's what it feels like.

*sits very still, tears forming in eyes*

I think about the vaccines again. They kept pushing them, and I said yes because... because I was afraid.

*voice becomes shaky*

Not afraid of the vaccines themselves. I'm afraid of being seen as someone who doesn't care about her baby. I'm afraid of being the mother who refused medical care. I'm afraid that if something happened to my baby, they would say it's because I refused the vaccines.

*wipes eyes*

But nobody really sat down and explained to me why I needed them. Nobody said, "Adela, here's what this vaccine does, here's why it's safe, here's what could happen if you don't get it." They just... expected me to understand and agree.

*voice becomes more raw*

And that's the thing about not having real agency. You're not making an informed choice. You're just... complying. Out of fear. Out of shame. Out of desperation to be seen as a good mother.

*pauses, looking down at belly*

I think about my daughters. I want them to grow up knowing they have control over their bodies. That they can ask questions. That they can say no. That their voices matter.

*voice breaks*

But how do I teach them that when I don't even know it myself? When I'm sitting in a clinic, nodding at things I don't understand, accepting things I'm not sure about, because I'm too scared to speak up?

*looks up with deep sadness*

I'm teaching them, without meaning to, that your body is not really yours. That you do what you're told. That being quiet and obedient is safer than asking for what you need.

*pause*

And that breaks my heart.
What would trust in your care team look like at a moment like that — what would they need to do or say for you to feel genuinely supported?

*takes a deep breath, speaking more thoughtfully*

Trust... that's a big word for me right now.

*pauses, organizing thoughts*

First, they would need to slow down. Just... slow down. Not rush me through an appointment like I'm a box to check. Sit with me. Make eye contact. Say my name — "Adela, I want to talk to you about something important."

*voice becomes more specific*

Then they would explain things in a way I can actually understand. Not medical words thrown at me so fast. Simple words. Pictures maybe. And they would ask me, "Do you understand?" And they would wait for a real answer, not just a nod.

*voice strengthens slightly*

They would say, "This is your choice. You don't have to do anything you're not comfortable with. And if you say no, we won't judge you. We'll just talk about it."

*pauses, getting more emotional*

And they would believe me when I say I'm scared. They wouldn't dismiss it. They would say, "Your fear is valid. Let's figure this out together."

*voice becomes quieter*

They would ask about my life. "Adela, how are you managing with two children at home? Do you have someone helping you? Are you eating enough? How are you sleeping?" Not as a checklist, but like they actually care about the answer.

*looks directly at you*

And most importantly... they would be consistent. The same person, or at least a small team I know. Someone who remembers my story. Who doesn't make me explain myself over and over.

*voice cracks slightly*

Someone who sees me as a whole person, not just a pregnant body with risk factors.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

*sits quietly for a long moment, then takes a shaky breath*

Yes. There is something.

*voice becomes very quiet but firm*

I want them to know that I'm trying. Every single day, I'm trying so hard. I'm working, I'm taking care of my daughters, I'm going to appointments, I'm taking the vitamins, I'm doing everything they tell me to do. Even when I don't understand. Even when I'm scared.

*eyes get watery*

And I want them to know that being a single mother is not a failure. It's not a "risk factor" that goes in my file. It's just my life. And I'm doing the best I can with what I have.

*pauses, voice becomes more emotional*

There's so much shame that comes with this. The way people look at me when I say I'm pregnant and there's no ring on my finger. The way I feel when I have to say "single mother" at the clinic. Like I've done something wrong.

*wipes eyes*

But I haven't done anything wrong. I'm just... a woman who loves her children. Who wants them to be healthy and safe. Who is doing everything she can, alone.

*voice becomes very small*

I also want to say... thank you. Thank you for listening to me. For not rushing. For asking real questions. For seeing me.

*pauses, looking at you with gratitude and lingering pain*

This is what I needed. Not more tests or pamphlets. Just someone to listen and care about what I'm actually going through.

*quietly*

I hope what I've shared helps other mothers like me feel less alone.
